title: Chapter 18: The Road
content: I come back to myself the way you surface from deep water — slowly, with pressure in my chest and light bleeding in from somewhere above.
Julian C is watching me.

He's always watching me when I return. I've started to recognize the particular set of his jaw, the way his fingers still on whatever they're holding, the brief flash of relief he buries before I can name it. He knows. Not the mechanics of it — not the glitching, not the multiverse, not the way I live five lives threaded through one body — but he knows something shifts. Something leaves and something comes back.
"You shift in your sleep," he told me once, weeks ago, back when I was still lying to myself about what was happening. "Your face changes. Like you're someone else for a second."
He wasn't wrong.
He's more right than he knows.

The café is dark after hours, chairs upended on tables, the espresso machine powered down to a low hum that sounds like a cat purring. Speaking of cats — Marmalade is on the counter, sprawled across one corner of the map Julian has spread between us. Smudge is perched on the till. The black and white one — Biscuit, I've finally learned — sits sentry by the door like she's working security.
"Where were you?" Julian asks. Not accusing. Just asking. The way you'd ask what someone dreamed about.
"Here," I say. It's not entirely a lie. "I'm here."

He holds my gaze for a long moment, then nods and turns back to the map of West Cork. The dock lights from the harbor throw amber stripes across his face through the window. His finger is already moving — tracing the coastline from Clonakilty south and east toward Cork City.

"Eamon has the evidence," he says. "Photos. The orders that came down before the incident. Everything that proves what actually happened. He's been sitting on it for three years, waiting for me to —" He stops. His jaw works. "Waiting for me to stop running, I suppose."
Eamon. A journalist from his army days. Someone Julian trusts, which is a list so short I could count it on one hand with fingers left over.
"He's in Cork City?"

"Montenotte. Near the university. He'll meet us at the Metropole if I can get a message through." Julian's finger traces the N71, the main road south and east. Then pulls away from it. "Harker will have the roads. Every checkpoint between here and Cork. He's been waiting for me to move."
"So we don't take the road."
His eyes flick up to mine. Something moves in them — amusement, maybe, or the ghost of it. "No. We don't."

He traces a different line. Not the road — the coast. His finger follows the jagged shoreline from Clonakilty Bay out past the Old Head of Kinsale, around the coast and up into Cork Harbor. He traces it the way someone else might trace a scar. Slow. Deliberate. Like he's remembering every mile of it.
"Declan has a boat. The Niamh O. She's small but she's sound. He owes me."

Declan. The café regular with the weathered face and the habit of ordering the same tea four times a morning because he keeps forgetting he's already ordered it. I've seen Julian slip extra scones into his bag when Declan's not looking, always casual, always deniable.
"What kind of favor does a café owner cash in with a fisherman?"

Julian's expression closes, then opens again. A door on a hinge. "His daughter. She was having trouble at school — being targeted, you know the way it goes. I helped her get placed somewhere safer. It wasn't anything."
It wasn't anything. The most generous lie in the English language.

"The sea route is longer," he says. "And it's not safe — Harker could have patrols on the water too, and if the weather turns—" He glances at the window. The night is clear, stars pressed flat against the dark like thumbtacks in velvet. "But it's the only option I can see."
I look at the route his finger has drawn. I look at him. The weight of what he's asking is written in the rigid line of his shoulders, the tension in his hands.
"When do we leave?"
"Now." He stands. "Every hour we wait is an hour Harker tightens the net."
I'm already reaching for my jacket when he catches my hand.

His fingers close around mine. Not grabbing — holding. The difference is everything. His hand is rough and warm and slightly trembling, and the tremor undoes me more than anything else he's done tonight. Julian C, who hasn't flinched at anything — not Harker, not the army, not three years of running — his hand shakes holding mine.
"I've been running alone for three years," he says. "Since the discharge. Before that, in the army, alone is how you survived. Trust got people killed."
He looks down at our hands. His thumb moves across my knuckles — barely, like he's reading braille.
"I don't know how to do this." His voice is low. Unsteady in a way I've never heard from him. "Whatever this is. But I know I don't want to do it alone anymore."

It's not a confession of love. Not yet. It's rawer than that. More dangerous. Love you can dress up, you can romanticize, you can stage-manage until it's safe. But need? Need is the exposed wire. Need is the truth you can't take back once it's out. Because need can be betrayed. Need means someone has the power to destroy you, and you're handing it to them anyway.
Julian C is handing it to me anyway.
I close my fingers around his. "You're not alone."
Three AM. The world has gone black and silver.

Declan's boat is a thirty-foot fishing vessel that smells of diesel and brine and something older — the accumulated salt-decay of a thousand catches, a thousand mornings pulling life from the water. The Niamh O. Her name is painted on the bow in white letters gone soft at the edges, and when Declan starts the engine, the whole vessel shudders like an animal waking.
We pull away from the dock and the darkness swallows us whole.

The Irish coast is a shadow to our left — just the idea of land, a deeper black against the black sky, the faintest suggestion of cliffs and fields and sleeping villages. The sea opens to our right, vast and indifferent, ancient in the way that only the ocean is ancient. It doesn't care about us. It was here before we existed and it will be here after, and there's something unbearably comforting about that. The sea's indifference is the only honest thing I've encountered in five timelines.

Julian stands at the prow. His silhouette is sharp against the not-quite-light — shoulders squared, head up, scanning the horizon. Soldier's stance. The body remembering what the mind would rather forget. The wind presses his jacket flat against his back and lifts his hair and he looks like something out of a painting, something too stark and too beautiful to be real.

I grab the wool blanket from the supplies Declan tossed at us and make my way forward on unsteady legs. The deck rolls beneath me, slick with spray, and I grab the rail more than once. The engine rumbles through the hull, vibrating up through the soles of my boots, settling somewhere in my sternum like a second heartbeat.

I reach him. Lift the blanket and wrap it around both of us, pulling the wool across his shoulders and mine, creating a pocket of warmth in the freezing dark. He glances down at me. The wind has carved color into his cheekbones and his eyes are very bright.
"Hi," I say.
"Hi."

I lean my head against his shoulder. The blanket smells of wool and something floral — detergent, maybe, someone's idea of fresh linen — and beneath it I can smell him. Coffee and soap and the faint salt of spray on his skin. His body is warm and solid against mine.
His arm comes around me.

It's not a question. There's no hesitation, no awkward adjustment. His arm wraps around my shoulders and pulls me in the way you pull someone out of traffic — reflexive, protective, like breathing. He protects the way other people blink. It's not a decision. It's just what his body does.

And God, the intimacy of it. His arm around me on a fishing boat in the middle of the black Irish Sea at three in the morning — it's more intimate than any kiss I've had in any timeline. More intimate than the night with Julian B in his flat with the rain on the windows, because that was wanting. This is having. This is being held by someone who knows what it costs to hold on and is doing it anyway.
The sea spray hits my face. Cold and sharp and tasting of salt and diesel and the iron-tanged edge of deep water. I lick my lips and taste the Atlantic, and I have never felt more alive.
He talks.

Really talks — not the careful, measured sentences from the café, not the deflections and half-truths. The darkness and the engine noise and the cold strip him down until there's nothing left to hide behind.
"My mother thinks I'm dead."
He says it flatly. Like he's reading a weather report. But his arm tightens around me — barely, just a fraction — and I feel the admission in his body before I hear it in his voice.

"She's in Dublin. Ranelagh. I haven't spoken to her since the discharge. It was easier to let her think —" He stops. His throat works. "I told myself it was easier. For her. But it was easier for me. That's the truth of it."
"Julian—"

"She has a garden," he says, cutting me off. Not rudely — he needs to get through this, and if I speak he'll lose the thread. "She grows tomatoes. Every summer she grows tomatoes and they're always too small and too sour and she gives them to the neighbors anyway because she's proud of them." His voice cracks on the last word. Barely. A hairline fracture. He seals it shut.
The boat rises on a swell and drops. My stomach drops with it. The blanket presses warm against my side where our bodies share heat, cold on the exposed shoulder where the wind finds me.

"My unit," he says. "The lads I served with. They were ordered to testify against me. And they did. Because orders. Because that's how it works — you follow orders, even when they're wrong, even when you know what really happened, because the army is the army and you don't break ranks." He's quiet for a moment. "I don't blame them. That's the worst part. I don't blame them. I would have done the same thing in their position. That's what the uniform does. It makes cowards of decent men."
"You're not a coward."
"No," he agrees. "I'm the idiot who didn't follow orders in the first place."
The engine note changes — Declan adjusting the throttle. The hull settles into a different rhythm, lower and slower, and the vibration shifts from my sternum to the base of my spine.

"Her name was Sarah," Julian says. "The teacher. She taught English to refugee children. Syrian, mostly. Afghan. Kids who'd lost everything and she gave them stories. Gave them language." His voice has gone quiet. Reverent, almost. "The convoy was ambushed. She was in the vehicle behind mine. I went back."
"You went back."

"I went back. The order was to push through — leave the disabled vehicle, maintain formation, push through." He says it like he's reciting something. Like he's read the after-action report so many times it's become scripture. "I went back anyway. Got her out. Got three of her students out. The vehicle burned."
"That's not what the record says."

"No. The record says I disobeyed a direct order during combat operations, thereby endangering my unit. The record says I caused the destruction of a vehicle and the compromise of the convoy's position." His jaw tightens. "The record doesn't mention Sarah. Doesn't mention the children. They were redacted out of the report. Operational security."

The wind shifts. Spray hits us broadside — cold sting across my cheek, salt on my lips, the bitter diesel aftertaste of the exhaust trailing from Declan's wheelhouse. Julian reaches up without thinking and wipes the water from my face with his thumb. The gesture is so natural, so automatic, that I don't think he even knows he's done it.
"If you could go back," I say. "Would you do it differently?"
He doesn't hesitate. Not for a second.

"Every time. I would go back and do it again. Every single time. Even knowing what it cost." He looks at me. His eyes are dark in the dark, but there's something in them — a light that doesn't come from the coast or the stars or the running lights of the boat. It comes from somewhere inside him. Some furnace that three years of running alone hasn't been able to extinguish. "Even knowing it would destroy me. I would do the right thing."

That's the core of him. That's Julian Blake stripped to the bone. Not the running, not the hiding, not the careful mask of the café owner who smiles at tourists and feeds stray cats and slips scones into Declan's bag. The core of him is a man who would do the right thing even knowing it would cost him everything. Real nobility. Not the performative kind — not the kind that announces itself and waits for applause. The quiet kind. The kind that just does it, then pays the price, then does it again.
I press closer to him. He holds me tighter. The boat cuts through the black water and the coast breathes to our left and the sky is so full of stars it looks like a wound.
Cork Harbor. Dawn.

The sky has gone from black to gray, that particular Irish gray that isn't quite day and isn't quite night — something in between, something undecided, the world holding its breath. The lights of the city are visible now. Just. The faint amber glow of street lamps, the distant suggestion of buildings, the long skeletal arm of a crane somewhere along the docklands.
We're nearly there.
The engine sputters.
Then it dies.

The silence is enormous. After hours of engine drone and hull vibration, the sudden absence is a physical thing — a pressure, a vacuum. The boat rocks gently on the harbor swell, and without the forward momentum, we start to drift. The lights of Cork City shimmer on the water, close enough to see, too far to reach.

Julian straightens. "No. No, no, no—" He swears. Creatively. Extensively. In Irish. The words are unfamiliar but the rhythm is unmistakable — each curse a hammer striking the same anvil of frustration.
From the wheelhouse, Declan's weathered face appears. He's grinning. Actually grinning, the lines around his eyes crinkling like he's been told a joke.
"She does that sometimes," Declan says, utterly calm. "Give her a minute."
Julian shoots him a look that could curdle milk. Declan's grin widens.

We drift. The gray water laps against the hull. The pre-dawn light thickens, and the city sharpens — I can make out individual buildings now, the stack of a church, the long low line of the port. So close. Salt crusted in my hair and on my lips and in the folds of the blanket, which has gone damp with sea air but is still warm where it's pressed between us. Julian's arm is still around me. He hasn't moved it. Not once. Not through the talking, not through the silence, not through the engine dying. His arm is around me like it belongs there.
The engine coughs. Once. Twice.
Catches.

The Niamh O shudders back to life, and Declan guides her forward with the easy confidence of a man who always knew she'd start again. We limp toward the dock, slower now, the engine running rough, but running. The harbor opens around us — fishing boats at anchor, the skeletal masts of sailboats, the industrial architecture of a working port.
Julian exhales. His arm tightens around me — reflex, again, the body protecting what it's decided to protect — and then relaxes.
We're here. We made it.

And I sit in the bow of this borrowed fishing boat with salt crusted in my hair and engine vibration humming through my boots and this man's arm warm and solid around my shoulders, and the thought comes to me — not arriving gently, not announcing itself, but crashing through me like a wave breaking:
I would choose this timeline.

If I could only have one. If the glitching stopped, if the timelines collapsed, if I had to pick and live with it forever — I would choose this one. This man. This boat. This cold gray dawn and this salt on my lips and this arm around me, holding me like breathing.
I would choose him.
The thought is terrifying.

Because choosing means all the other timelines die. Choosing means the Julians in the other worlds — the ones I've kissed, the ones I've fought alongside, the one I made laugh so hard he spilled coffee on his shirt — they become ghosts. Hypotheticals. The lives I didn't live. And I don't know if I can survive that. I don't know if I can be the kind of person who closes doors, who commits, who says this one and means it and never looks back.
That's the thing I've never been able to do. In any timeline. In any version of my life. I've always kept one foot out the door. Always maintained the exit. Always been ready to glitch away.
But sitting here, with his heartbeat steady against my temple and the harbor lights reflected in the dark water, I know which one I'd choose.
I know.
But knowing and choosing aren't the same thing.
And I'm not sure I'll ever be brave enough to make them match.
